summary: McKenzie discovers that his earlier intuition about bots not affecting A/B test results was mathematically wrong. Through algebraic analysis, he shows that bot traffic artificially depresses z-scores, potentially turning statistically significant results into null results. He implements a JavaScript-based bot detection system for his open source A/B testing library A/Bingo, requiring clients to perform arithmetic via AJAX POST requests — something resource-intensive for bots but trivial for browsers. The system retroactively scores participation when a visitor first proves human, integrating cleanly with existing test tracking.
key_points: Bot traffic doesn't cancel out in A/B tests as intuition suggests — the math shows bots artificially depress z-scores by pushing conversion rates toward zero, making significant results appear insignificant
User-Agent filtering catches barely half of bots, making it inadequate as a detection mechanism
Requiring JavaScript execution via AJAX POST is an effective low-friction bot filter because executing JS at web scale is resource-intensive for bot operators
The implementation defers scoring participation until humanness is proven, then retroactively applies results to previously seen tests
The same technique has broader applications: interaction-free CAPTCHAs for comments, load-shedding non-human traffic under peak load, and blocking destructive bot actions
money_quotes: I love when the market gives me exactly what I want.
Bots do bork statistical significance testing, by artificially depressing z scores and thus turning statistically significant results into null results at the margin.
It is a very bad idea to force all users to prove that they are human just so that you can A/B test them.
This isn't to keep a dedicated adversary out, it is to distinguish the overwhelming majority of bots from humans. This isn't a security feature.
While it is not specifically designed that Richard Stallman and folks running NoScript can't influence my future development directions, I am not overwrought with grief at that coincidence.
I am a big believer in not spending time creating features until you know customers actually need them.

I gave him my stock answer for folks who disagree with me on OSS design directions: it is MIT licensed, so you can fork it and code the feature yourself. If you are too busy to code it, that is fine, I am available for consulting.
key_insight: Bot traffic doesn't harmlessly wash out of A/B tests — it mathematically depresses z-scores, and JavaScript execution challenges are an effective, low-friction way to filter bots without burdening real users.
